coredata.dc:description: Herein, we rationally designed a novel and forceful ciprofloxacin (CIP) grafted zinc-doped-carbon dots (CIP@Zn/CDs) as an efficient artificial nanozyme against Escherichia coli (E. coli), multi-drug-resistant E. coli (AREC), and E. coli biofilms through the peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic activity and the synergistic antibacterial activity of CIP and Zn2+. On the one hand, extracellular DNA (eDNA) cleave by high levels of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) catalyzed by the POD-like catalytic activity of CIP@Zn/CDs. On the other hand, CIP@Zn/CDs with positive surface potential, elimination abilities against E. coli via electrostatic interaction, changing the cell membrane permeability and eventually leading to E. coli death within E. coli biofilms. Furthermore, the ultrasmall size of CIP@Zn/CDs possesses good biocompatibility favoring clinical translation. This work provides potential to develop CD-based techniques for applications in the field of selective antibacterial agents towards Gram-negative bacteria.

head.ce:title: Ciprofloxacin-based zinc-doped carbon dots with peroxidase-like activity against Escherichia coli and biofilm eradication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we rationally designed a novel and forceful ciprofloxacin (CIP) grafted zinc-doped-carbon dots (CIP@Zn/CDs) as an efficient artificial nanozyme against Escherichia coli (E. coli), multi-drug-resistant E. coli (AREC), and E. coli biofilms through the peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic activity and the synergistic antibacterial activity of CIP and Zn2+. On the one hand, extracellular DNA (eDNA) cleave by high levels of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) catalyzed by the POD-like catalytic activity of CIP@Zn/CDs. On the other hand, CIP@Zn/CDs with positive surface potential, elimination abilities against E. coli via electrostatic interaction, changing the cell membrane permeability and eventually leading to E. coli death within E. coli biofilms. Furthermore, the ultrasmall size of CIP@Zn/CDs possesses good biocompatibility favoring clinical translation. This work provides potential to develop CD-based techniques for applications in the field of selective antibacterial agents towards Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne pathogen, which posing a severe threaten to public health, are one of the most important problems of the food industry (Park et al., 2022). Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a representative foodborne pathogen and is considered the third leading cause of disease in the world (Shi et al., 2021; Kogularasu et al., 2022). Antibiotics are an effective treatment, however more and more bacteria have developed antimicrobial resistance (AMR) because of the abuse of antibiotics (Zheng et al., 2019). The World Health Organization (WHO) annunciated a list of priority pathogens that are resistant to antibiotics in 2018, most of which are Gram-negative (G−) bacteria (Govindaraj Vaithinathan and Vanitha, 2018). For the distinct shielded structure of G− bacteria by the outer membrane, most obvious the formation of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (Huwaitat et al., 2016; Qiao et al., 2022), G− bacteria are more sensitive to AMR than Gram-positive (G+) bacteria and cause serious morbidity and mortality worldwide (Breijyeh et al., 2020). Therefore, designing novel and effective, biocompatible antimicrobials becomes highly required.

Nanozyme (a class of nanomaterials with similar natural enzyme activity) as a novel type of antibacterial agents have proved their great potential against pathogenic bacteria (Yang et al., 2020, 2021; Wang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). Generally, the artificial nanozymes-based antibacterial agents show the capacity to catalyze the reaction for producing reactive oxygen species (ROS, e.g. •OH), exerting toxic actions on bacteria and thus avoiding the generation of bacterial resistance (Martin and Barrett, 2002; Trachootham et al., 2009). At present, various nanozymes have been applied for antibacterial therapy, such as metal-based nanozymes (Fang et al., 2018; Xi et al., 2019), metal oxides-based or metal sulfides -based nanozymes (Matsuda et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2023), and carbon-based nanozymes (Sun et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2018). Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have aroused general concern as new antimicrobial alternatives due to their simple synthesis, microbial adhesion, low toxicity, and catalytic properties, and especially good biocompatibility features (Xin et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2020). In particular, some researchers have shown the nanozymes activities of CDs especially metals doping CDs with higher utilization of active sites throughout the peroxidase (POD)-like catalytic process, against pelagic bacteria and disrupt bacterial biofilms (Gao et al., 2007; Li et al., 2021; Unnikrishnan et al., 2021). Zn nanoparticles (Zn-NPs) have been considered multifunctional nanomaterials because of their antibacterial ability, piezoelectricity, and near-ultraviolet emission (Monfared-Hajishirkiaee et al., 2023). It has been generally thought that introducing Zn-NPs to biopolymer film can overcome the restrictions of weak antibacterial property (Satriaji et al., 2020; Roy et al., 2021; Safardoust-Hojaghan et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2023). Therefore, Zn doping CDs can be a good strategy (Liu et al., 2021; Reczkowski et al., 2024; Tariq et al., 2024). Ciprofloxacin (CIP) as one of the quinolones, has a broad spectrum and strong antibacterial effect. However, the clinical use of CIP is limited by drug resistance. It has been reported that copper-doped CIP carbon dots (Cu-CCDs) are used for antibacterial (Miao et al., 2023). This study also only used the performance of Cu-CCDs, and did not research the simulate enzyme performance of Cu-CCDs. In addition, zinc-doped carbon dots (Z-CDs) have been used for antibacterial studies, but their enzyme-mimicking properties remain unexplored (Divsalar et al., 2023).

In this work, zinc-doped CIP carbon dots (CIP@Zn/CDs) were successfully synthesized via a facile one-pot microwave method. These carbon dots exhibited an average size of approximately 3.46 nm and contained surface amino groups, which endowed them with a positive zeta potential of +9.18 mV and POD-like enzymatic activity. The antibacterial activity and mechanism of CIP@Zn/CDs was explored using pathogenic E. coli and AREC as model microorganisms. This design utilized the synergistic effects of CIP and Zn2+ targeting bacterial cell membranes, positively charged CIP@Zn/CDs attached to negatively charged bacteria through electrostatic interactions, and POD-like activity effectively catalyzed the production of toxic •OH from H2O2, and thus resulting in destruction of the bacterial membrane structure and causing the leakage of intracellular mate rial that ultimately killed the bacteria (Fig. 1 ). This work aimed to provide a new effective control of E. coli and AREC in food and biomedicine fields to replace antibiotics and avoid drug resistance.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) was sourced from Xilong Science Co., Ltd. (China). Crystal violet (CV) and hydrogen peroxide (30 wt%) were acquired from National Pharmaceutical Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and methylene blue (MB) were obtained from Tianjin Hynes Bio-chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Propidium iodide (PI) and SYTO-9 green fluorescent nucleic acid stain were obtained from Symer Fisher Technologies Ltd. (USA). 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and CIP were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). N-Phenyl-1-napthyl amine (NPN), Methanol and glacial acetic acid (HAc) were sourced from McLin Biotech, Inc. (China). The strains of Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC-8099) and ampicillin-resistant Escherichia coli (AREC, SHBCC D25148) were obtained from the Shanghai Bioresource Collection Center. Culture media for these strains, including Luria Bertani (LB) medium, were procured from Beijing Land Bridge Technology Co., Ltd. and were stored at 25 °C.

At 200 kV, a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan) was used transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures were captured. An ULVAC-PHI.INC type PHI5000 (VersaprobeIII XPS, Japan) was used to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Use UV-2550 (Shimadzu, Japan) and G9800A Cary Eclipse (Agilent Technologies, USA) fluorescence spectrophotometers to measure the corresponding UV vis and fluorescence absorption spectra of the material. The results of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) are based on data obtained from a TENSOR27 analytical spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is derived from the results displayed by D8-advavanve (Bruker, Germany). The morphology of E. coli and AREC were investigated by Sanning electron microscope S-4800 FE-SEM (Hitachi, Japan). Meanwhile, laser confocal scanning microscopy (CLSM) (Leica, Germany) was used to observe the staining of bacteria. Additionally, an electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker EMX plus, Germany) was used to evaluate the scavenging activity of •OH.

In brief, 0.01 g CIP and 0.04 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were added into 30 mL DI water. After ultrasound for 30 min, then the mixture was quickly put into a Teflon reactor, and the temperature was risen to 180 °C in 850 W microwave irradiations for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove solid particles and obtained CIP@Zn/CDs. The same synthesis procedure of CIP@CDs and Zn/CDs was performed without adding Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and CIP, respectively.

The method previously outlined was used to calculate the quantum yield (QY) of CIP@Zn/CDs (Asha Jhonsi and Thulasi, 2016). To summarize, the standard was a quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY of 54 % at 360 nm, η = 1.33). The quantum yield is calculated by the following formula:
Q =

here, Q represents the QY, the subscript ‘S’ represents the sample to be tested, and ‘R’ in the formula represents quinine sulfate. A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the corresponding substance, I represent the integrated emission intensity, and η is the solvent refraction index.

The POD-like activity was thoroughly assessed through typical chromogenic feedback using TMB treat as base. In the experiment, 0.5 mg/mL CIP@Zn/CDs solution with 200 μL TMB (5 mM) chromogenic substrate and 100 μL H2O2 (50 mM) solution. Dilute the mixture to 3 mL with deionized water and react at room temperature for 10 min. The absorbance changes at various concentrations of CIP@Zn/CDs were monitored at a wavelength of 652 nm using a UV–vis spectrometer. To investigate the kinetics of the nanozymes, different concentrations of H2O2 and TMB were added sequentially. Measure the changes in UV absorbance at 652 nm over a period of 300 s. Determine dynamic parameters using Michaelis Menten curve and Lineweaver Burk reciprocal plot:
1 / V =
K m
/
V max
× 1 /
[ S ]
+ 1 /
V max
here, v signifies the reaction rate, [S] represents the substrate concentration (TMB, H2O2), K m denotes the Michaelis constant, and V max indicates the maximal reaction rate.

MB served as an indicator for detecting •OH by monitoring changes in absorbance upon mixing with CIP@Zn/CDs and H2O2. Specifically, a 5 mM MB solution (25 μL) was combined with 200 μL of 50 mM H2O2 and 60 μL of 0.5 mg/mL CIP@Zn/CDs in phosphate buffer, then left to incubate for 4 h at room temperature without light. The emitted absorbance was inversely related to the •OH content. Additionally, •OH was investigated using ESR. The mixture of CIP@Zn/CDs, H2O2 and DMPO merge with phosphate buffer (pH 3.0) and then transferred to a glass capillary for ESR analysis to record the •OH signal.

The ROS standards were determined applying a dye-based probe, DCFH-DA. Specifically, a 20 mM DCFH-DA solution (100 μL) was mixed with CIP@Zn/CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 100 μL) and H2O2 (250 mM, 10 μL) in phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) and hatched at 37 °C for a duration of 30 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was measured applying a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 280 nm and an emission wavelength of 450 nm.

Using the plate scribing technique, E. coli and AREC were inoculated onto agar plates and allowed to grow into single colonies while being incubated at 37 °C. To make a bacterial suspension, these colonies were subsequently moved to sterile saline (0.9 % NaCl). A Mackenzie turbidimeter was used to quantify the suspension's turbidity, which was adjusted to 0.5, yielding a concentration measured at 1 × 105 CFU/mL.

In vitro antibacterial experiments, the prepared suspensions of E. coli and AREC, originally at a concentration of 1 × 108 CFU/mL, were reduced to 1 × 105 CFU/mL using sterile phosphate buffer through the plate counting method. The Kirby-Bauer method was then employed for the disk diffusion assay. The materials were divided into seven groups: control, H2O2, CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2, CIP@CDs + H2O2, Zn@CDs + H2O2, CIP, antibacterial drug (gentamicin). The control group of E. coli and AREC incubated in PBS buffer was used as a reference. For minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) estimation, selected nanozyme and bacteria suspensions were spread plated onto LB solid medium and nurtured in 37 °C for 24 h. The concentration at which no E. coli and AREC colonies appeared on LB solid medium was referred as MBC. For the plate counting method, the suspensions of E. coli and AREC were diluted, and 100 μL of the diluted suspensions with varying concentrations of CIP@Zn/CDs were spread onto LB solid medium. The plates were then nurtured in 37 °C for 24 h. The antimicrobial rate was calculated based on the number of colonies observed. To estimate the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), 1 μL of E. coli and AREC suspension (OD600 = 0.6), 900 μL of liquid medium, and different concentrations of CIP@Zn/CDs (0.39, 0.78, 1.56, 3.16, 6.25, and 12.5 μg/mL) were mixed in a 96-well plate. The plates were nurtured in a shaker at 37 °C for 24 h, and the OD600 values were measured using a microplate reader.

Propidium iodide (PI) uptake assay was applied to test the perform of CIP@Zn/CDs on the membrane permeability barrier of E. coli and AREC. Briefly, E. coli and AREC suspensions (1 × 108 CFU/mL) cells were exposed into four groups: control, CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2, CIP@CDs + H2O2, Zn@CDs + H2O2, respectively. E. coli and AREC in first group without any treatment were worked as a control group. E. coli and AREC in the second and third group were treated with CIP@CDs (10 μg/mL) along with H2O2 (50 μM) and Zn/CDs (10 μg/mL) for 3 h. In the fourth group, E. coli and AREC were treated with CIP@Zn/CDs (10 μg/mL) combined with H2O2 for the same duration. The suspensions from all four groups were co-stained with SYTO-9 (1.0 mg/mL, 10 μL) and PI (1.0 mg/mL, 10 μL) for 15 min in a dark environment. The suspensions were then washed several times with sterile PBS buffer to eliminate any excess dyes. Finally, using confocal laser scanning microscopy, pictures of the live/dead cell labeling were taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological observation of bacterial cell membrane integrity
ce:section.ce:para: After centrifuging the suspension of E. coli and AREC (1 × 108 CFU/mL) for 5 min at 4000 rpm, PBS was used six times. Afterward, the E. coli and AREC suspensions were placed on a slide, and the samples were fixed with a 4 % glutaraldehyde solution for 4 h. The E. coli and AREC were then gradually dehydrated using ethanol solutions at focus of 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, and 100 %, with each concentration applied for 10 min. The E. coli and AREC were permitted to air dry at indoor temperature for 12 h. Finally, they were captured using SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effect of nanozyme on bacterial biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: The cultivation of bacterial biofilms was conducted using the following method. Initially, 10 μL of E. coli and AREC suspension (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was mixed with 990 μL of liquid medium in a 24-well plate and held at 37 °C for 12 h. After incubation, each well's growth medium was carefully removed, and the biofilm was flushed from the bottom 2–3 times using PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The resulting E. coli and AREC biofilm was then dealt with H2O2, CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2, CIP@CDs + H2O2, Zn/CDs + H2O2 and gentamicin each at concentrations of CIP@Zn/CDs and H2O2 of 10 μg/mL and 50 mM, respectively, and cultured at 37 °C for 48 h. CV staining was employed to assess the inhibitory effect of CIP@Zn/CDs on the biofilm. First, 100 μL of methanol was added to each well for fixation. After air drying naturally, each well received 300 μL of CV solution (0.1 %), which was then incubated for 20 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of 33 % acetic acid solution was added to each well and shaken for 30 min to resolve the CV. Finally, the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 600 nm using a microplate reader.

The biocompatibility of nanozymes was determined by CCK-8 assay. Briefly, the cytotoxicity of CIP@Zn/CDs on human hepatoma cell line (Hep G2) and human umbilical vein endothelial cell (HUVECs) was tested by CCK-8 assay. Hep G2 and HUVECs with 5 × 103 cells/well were then added to 96-well plates and then cultured in a 37 °C incubator with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Three different concentrations of CIP@Zn/CDs nanozyme were mixed and cultured for 48 h. The cultured cells were washed with sterile PBS buffer to take out suspended dead cells, impurities and nanomaterials. CCK-8 solution (10 μL) was added and the compound was developed again in a 37 °C incubator for 3 h, and the cytotoxicity size was assessed using absorbance at 450 nm recorded by a microplate reader.

The data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Unpaired Student's t-tests were employed to contrast the means of two different assemblages. For comparisons involving three or more assemblages, one-way examine of variance with the addition of the Newman-Keuls exam was conducted. The sense levels are indicated as follows: ∗, P < 0.05; ∗∗, P < 0.01; ∗∗∗, P < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗, P < 0.0001; ns, not significant (P > 0.05).

To ensure the retention of the antibacterial functional groups while simultaneously doping with zinc atoms, CIP and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were selected as precursors via a one-step microwave synthesis approach. The TEM images of CIP@Zn/CDs, shown in Fig. 2 a and b, indicate that the average size of the nanoparticles is roughly 3.46 nm. The lattice fringes observed were uniformly structured with a spacing of 0.22 nm, which aligns with the (002) facet of graphitic carbon. The XRD pattern of CIP@Zn/CDs displayed a prominent peak at approximately 23.9°, corresponding to graphitic carbon as depicted in Fig. 2c (Huang et al., 2023). CIP, a quinolone antibacterial agent, possesses available functional groups including carboxyl and quinoline (Fallone et al., 2019). The FT-IR spectrum of CIP@Zn/CDs exhibited characteristic peaks at 730.9, 1384.7 and 1488.3 cm−1, which consistent with C-N, C=C, and C-H bonds in benzene, confirming the preservation of the CIP quinoline group (Fig. 2d). Additionally, significant absorption peaks at 1624.3 and 3470 cm−1 indicated the presence of -COOH in CIP. Furthermore, a Zn-O bond was detected at approximately 812.1 cm−1, suggesting the formation of a complex between oxygen and the metal (Das et al., 2018). According to the XPS data presented in Fig. 2e, CIP@Zn/CDs contained the following elemental composition: C (42.02 %), O (41.39 %), N (2.64 %), F (2.07 %), and Zn (11.89 %). The C 1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 288.4, 286.4, 290.0, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C=O, C-O, C-F, and C-C, respectively (Fig. 2f). In the N 1s spectrum, two peaks were attributed to graphitic nitrogen and amino nitrogen (Fig. 2g). The O 1s spectrum exhibited signals for C=O (531.2 eV), -OH (534.0 eV), and C-O (532.5 eV) (Fig. 2h), with the C-F signal (687.2 eV) also detected in the F 1s spectrum (Fig. 2i). Notably, the Zn 2p spectrum presented peaks at 1045.4 eV and 1022.3 eV, which were ascribed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. 2j). In both cases, the energy difference between these two peaks was 23.1 eV, indicating the Zn2+ oxidation state (Li et al., 2015; Tian et al., 2017).

CIP@Zn/CDs possessed both POD-like and inherent fluorescence properties. Fig. 3 a presents the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CIP@Zn/CDs. The synthesized CIP@Zn/CDs displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 276 nm, which is attributed to the π→π∗ move of the aromatic sp2 domains. The spectra also exhibited a extremum fluorescence emission at 450 nm when excited at 280 nm, and this emission wavelength remained consistent across various excitation wavelengths (Roy et al., 2015) (Fig. 3b). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CIP@Zn/CDs enhanced as its concentration increased (Fig. 3c). Fig. 3d exhibited the CIP@Zn/CDs emitted a stronger fluorescence than CIP@CDs and Zn@CDs attributing to CIP interacting with Zn2+. With quinine sulfate as the standard fluorescence reference, the showed a fluorescence quantum yield of CIP@Zn/CDs (14.32 %) > Zn@CDs (8.14 %) > CIP@CDs (1.4 %).

The mimic enzyme activity of nanozymes were used TMB as the chromogenic substrate. Nanozymes can catalyze the colorless TMB to blue oxidized oxTMB at 652 nm (Fig. 4 a) with H2O2. The results showed CIP@Zn/CDs had excellent POD-like activity, and the order of reaction rate of the catalysis is CIP@Zn/CDs > CIP@CDs > Zn@CDs. Therefore, the catalytic activity was significantly improved with the incorporation of a small amount of Zn.

Steady-state kinetic analysis was performed to investigate the catalytic mechanism of CIP@Zn/CDs. Under ideal circumstances for the catalytic oxidation of TMB and H2O2, the typical Michaelis-Menten curves and accompanying Lineweaver-Burk plots for CIP@Zn/CDs and CIP@CDs were analyzed. Further analysis allowed for the determination of the fitting curves for the Michaelis constant (K m) and maximum initial velocity (V max) (Fig. 4b–e). Accordingly, the K m and V max values of CIP@Zn/CDs and CIP@CDs were displayed in Table 1 . Compared to the CIP@CDs, CIP@Zn/CDs exhibited smaller values of K m (TMB: 0.18 mM, H2O2: 6.03 mM), showing better affinity toward H2O2 and TMB. Moreover, the values of V max of CIP@Zn/CDs (TMB: 2.56×10− 8 M s−1, H2O2: 5.16×10− 8 M s−1) were highest than CIP@CDs, showing more fast catalytic rates.

ROS plays a critical involve in antibacterial activity. The generated •OH was determined using MB degradation, which can produce the absorbance decrease at 665 nm. As shown in Fig. 4f, the absorbance of the MB was significantly lower in CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2, suggesting more •OH generation. In addition, the •OH generation in the reaction system was further detected by ESR. As shown in Fig. 4g, the apparent •OH signal peak was detected under the condition of CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2. It further proved that •OH was produced under the catalysis of CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2.

The biotoxicity of the nanozymes were evaluated by assaying the viability of cells (HUVECs and Hep G2 cells). Specifically, cells were incubated exposed to different nanozymes (CIP@CDs, Zn@CDs, and CIP@Zn/CDs) and different gradients MBC (MBC = 10 μg/mL) at same condition for 48 h. Cytotoxicity was assessed by measuring the cell survival rate. The results indicated that the survival rate of HUVECs and Hep G2 cells was approximately 100 % for CIP@Zn/CDs after 48 h of incubation (Fig. 5 a and b), demonstrating that CIP@Zn/CDs exhibits excellent biocompatibility.

Then, to investigate the antibacterial effect of nanozymes, the E. coli and AREC were adopted as models to compare the antibacterial ability of CIP@Zn/CDs with those of pure CIP, CIP@CDs and Zn@CDs. And the antibacterial activity results treated with H2O2, CIP@CDs, Zn@CDs, CIP@Zn/CDs (0.03 μg/mL), CIP and gentamicin were shown in (Fig. 5c) by using LB medium test. It was clear that lots of bacterial colonies were observed in the CIP@CDs, Zn@CDs, CIP and H2O2 groups, indicating not antimicrobial effect toward E. coli and AREC. However, an obvious inhibition effect was observed in the CIP@Zn/CDs group with no colonies, with MBC of 10 μg/mL against E. coli and AREC (Fig. 5d). And the comparison of MBC concentration of different nanozymes is shown in Table 2 This might be due to the synergism of doping of Zn atoms and active functional groups of CIP in CDs and POD-like activity of CIP@Zn/CDs (Fig. 5e). It was found that the inhibition rate of CIP@Zn/CDs against E. coli and AREC could reach 99.9 %. At the same concentration, CIP@CDs and Zn@CDs groups had no obvious inhibitory effect on E. coli and AREC (Fig. 5f and g). And in the above experiments, the 50 μM H2O2 had no significant bactericidal effect on E. coli and AREC (Fig. 5h). Then, the E. coli and AREC survival rate was estimated by comparing OD600 values of the E. coli and AREC suspension. Bacterial concentrations for standard strain of E. coli and AREC (1 × 105 CFU/mL) in induction of different concentrations CIP@Zn/CDs (1.56–12.5 μg/mL) + H2O2 (50 μM) and gentamicin were shown in Fig. 5i. The MIC of CIP@Zn/CDs was 1.56 μg/mL, which was better than that of gentamicin. In addition, the results of zeta potential measurement (Fig. 5j) showed that the surface zeta potential of CIP@Zn/CDs was 9.18 ± 1.82 mV, and readily mixed by electrostatic contact with negatively charged E. coli and AREC, changing the permeability of cell membranes (Chung and Chen, 2008).

Using CV staining, the antibiofilm efficacy of CIP@Zn/CDs was assessed, and the biofilm was quantified by measuring the absorbance at 590 nm. As shown in Fig. 6 a, b comparing with the control group, the CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2 groups significantly inhibited the formation of the biofilms of E. coli and AREC, as it showed the lowest biofilm survival rate of 19.77 % and 44.93 %.

Live and dead bacterial staining experiments, along with quantified fluorescence intensity, were conducted using SYTO-9/PI. In this method, live microbes were stained green, while dead bacteria appeared red (Hu et al., 2020). As illustrated in Fig. 6c, with no red fluorescence from PI detected, the bacterial cells in the control group showed high green fluorescence, suggesting that most of the bacteria were still alive and only a small number had perished. Nevertheless, almost all E. coli and AREC are stained red in the company of CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2, indicating successful eradication of E. coli and AREC. The results indicate that the CIP@Zn/CDs with the stimulation of H2O2 showed the excellent antibacterial ability by disrupting bacterial cell membranes. Subsequently, we conducted ROS staining on E. coli and AREC following various treatments to assess intracellular ROS levels. As shown in Fig. 6d, slight green fluorescence indicative of ROS generation was observed in E. coli and AREC dealt with H2O2, CIP@CDs + H2O2, and Zn@CDs + H2O2, suggesting that these treatments did not produce sufficient ROS to effectively kill the bacteria. In contrast, the CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2 treatment exhibited significantly stronger green fluorescence signals compared to the other groups, indicating a higher production of ROS for antibacterial action. To further evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of CIP@Zn/CDs, SEM images of E. coli and AREC dealt with CIP@CDs + H2O2, Zn@CDs + H2O2, and CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2 were obtained. Fig. 6e demonstrates that the cell membranes of E. coli and AREC in the CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2 group displayed folds, shrinkage, and complete disruption, losing their original morphology. Additionally, bacterial external membrane permeability was assessed using the hydrophobic fluorescent probe NPN, which shows weak fluorescence in aqueous solutions and strong fluorescence in hydrophobic environments (Mellegård et al., 2011). The fluorescence intensity of NPN in bacteria treated with CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2 is illustrated in Fig. 6f and g. The results indicated that CIP@Zn/CDs + H2O2 treatment significantly enhanced outer membrane permeabilization, with fluorescence intensities increasing to varying degrees. Another indicator of bacterial membrane disruption was the leakage of intracellular materials, including essential proteins. As depicted in Fig. 6h, the Bicinchoninic Acid Assay (BCA) was employed to quantify protein leakage in bacteria after CIP@Zn/CDs treatment. Under alkaline conditions, the protein reduced Cu2+ to Cu+, which then reacted with BCA molecules to form a purple complex. Collectively, these findings highlight the exceptional bactericidal properties of CIP@Zn/CDs, driven by their POD-like activity and the synergistic antibacterial effects of CIP and Zn2+against Gram-negative bacteria.

G− bacteria have become a very problematic issue in clinical infections because of their unique structure and tendency to develop drug-resistant bacteria and even biofilms. So, a CIP@Zn/CDs was constructed for selective against gram-negative bacteria E. coli and AREC. The prepared CIP@Zn/CDs not only maintained the antibacterial properties of ZnNPs (non-ZnO), but also had POD-like activity. The CIP@Zn/CDs with positive charge showed POD-like activity to catalyze the generation of •OH and excellent fluorescence properties. The antibacterial mechanism contain CIP@Zn/CDs enter the interior of bacteria by electrostatic interaction, altering the cell membrane permeability and causing cell death within G− bacteria biofilms. This work provides a new synthesis route of Zn doped CDs, which will promote their great bio-application as promising alternative antibacterial agents with biocompatibility, inhibition of resistance development.

Zizhuo Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Jinya Wei: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jitao Li: Investigation. Yaling Yang: Validation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Dezhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yuzhu Song: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology.
